title: The Money Game Poor People Don't Know Exists
full_text: Today we look at the question of wealth. Okay. The question is why do poor people misunderstand wealth and why does this misunderstanding keep them poor? Okay. So we we've done a lot of research and we've discovered that people who who understand wealth um understand it for certain reasons. Those who don't understand it often think they do. That's what what's called survivorship bias. And this is this helps explain why financial advice doesn't work because we only study rich people who succeeded. We ignore all the rich people who failed. Okay? Like for every Warren Buffett, there's a thousand people who invested the same way and went bankrupt. But we don't write books about them. So all our financial advice comes from survivors. And survivor advice doesn't work for people who aren't survivors yet. Okay. Okay. You guys, you guys need to remember this. Just because rich people do certain things does not mean doing those things makes you rich. So, I'll give you an example. We know that wealthy people, they invest in the stock market. Okay? They have diversified portfolios. But just because you invest in the stock market does not mean you'll become wealthy. Okay? Just because you read financial books does not mean you'll build wealth. But if you're wealthy, you will invest in the stock market because you have money you don't need for survival. Okay, does that make sense? So if you're rich, guess what happens? You become wealthy and you'll understand compound interest. Okay, does that make sense? Okay, so the question for us is why does this happen? And ultimately, what's the difference between being rich and understanding wealth? Okay. So, we know for a fact that people who understand wealth are much more likely to build generational wealth than people who don't understand it. And in fact, what we know from economic studies is that financial literacy doesn't really matter. Doesn't matter how much you know about money. If you don't understand wealth, you'll stay poor. If you understand wealth, you'll build generational fortune. Okay? So, what's the difference? Okay. So now let's look at the fundamental differences between how poor people think about money and how rich people think about wealth. Okay. So there are lots of differences but let's focus on three major differences. The first major difference is what I call time horizon blindness. Okay. So poor people when they think about wealth they think wealth is about having money now. right now today. Okay. If you have $10,000 in your bank account, a poor person thinks I am rich. Okay? But if you're a rich person and you have $10,000 in your bank account, you think this is nothing. This is basically zero. Does that make sense? So here's the key difference. Poor people see wealth as an amount, a number. I have X dollars, therefore I'm wealthy. But rich people see wealth as independence from labor. Okay? So when a rich person says, "I'm wealthy," they don't mean, "I have a lot of money." They mean, "I never have to work again and my lifestyle doesn't change." Okay? So a poor person with $10,000 thinks they're temporarily rich. A rich person with $10,000 thinks they're basically broke because that$10,000 doesn't buy them time. Does that make sense? And this goes back to time horizons. Poor people think about money in terms of weeks or months. Can I pay rent this month? Can I buy groceries this week? Okay. Rich people think about money in terms of forever. Will this asset generate income for my grandchildren? Okay. So, poor people optimize for having money now. Rich people optimize for controlling time forever. And because they're thinking on completely different time scales, they make completely different decisions. A poor person saves money in a bank account because they might need it next month. A rich person would never save money in a bank account because that money is not working for them. It's not buying them more time. Okay. The second major difference is what I call asset versus income confusion. So if you think that if you have a high salary, you're wealthy, right? Like a doctor making $300,000 a year. Poor people look at that and think, "Wow, that doctor is rich." Okay? But rich people look at that same doctor and think that doctor is a highincome worker, but they're not wealthy. Does that make sense? Here's why. The doctor makes $300,000 a year, but they have to work to make that money. If the doctor stops working, the money stops. Okay? So, the doctor has a high income, but they're not wealthy. Now, compare that to a landlord who owns apartment buildings that generate $100,000 a year in rent. The landlord makes less money than the doctor, but the landlord is wealthier. Why? because the landlord doesn't have to work for that h 100,000. The buildings generate money while the landlord sleeps. Okay, so here's the key misunderstanding. Poor people optimize for income. They want a higher salary, a better paying job, a promotion, a raise. Rich people optimize for equity and ownership. They want things that generate money without their labor. Okay? So poor people save cash in a bank. Rich people buy things that make more money. Poor people work for money. Rich people make money work for them. Does that make sense? And this is why when you tell a poor person, you should invest, they don't really understand what that means. To them, investing means putting money somewhere they can't touch it. It feels like losing money. But to a rich person, investing means buying something that will generate more money. It feels like planting seeds. Okay. completely different mindset. The poor person sees an expense. The rich person sees an asset. Okay. The third major difference is what I call risk perception inversion. Okay. Risk perception. So poor people think that having a job is safe and starting a business is risky. Right? Everyone knows this. Get a good job, that's the safe path. starting a business that's risky. You might lose everything. Okay? But rich people think the exact opposite. Rich people think depending on one income source is risky. Having only a job is dangerous, but having multiple income streams, that's safe. Okay? So, let me give you an example. A poor person has a job that pays $50,000 a year. They're terrified of quitting because if they quit, they have zero income. So, they stay at the job even if they hate it, even if it's going nowhere because the alternative is no income. That feels risky. Okay. But a rich person looks at that same situation and thinks, "Oh my god, you only have one income source. What if you get fired? What if the company goes bankrupt? What if you get sick? You're one bad event away from zero income. That's terrifying. Does that make sense? So, poor people optimize for stability in poverty. They want a steady paycheck, benefits, predictability, even if it means staying poor. At least it's stable poverty. Rich people optimize for volatility with a safety net. They'll take risks, start businesses, make investments that might fail because even if they fail, they have other income sources. They have assets. They have a safety net. Okay? And this is why when rich people give advice like just start a business or invest in yourself or take risks. Poor people think that's crazy advice because for a poor person, taking a risk means potential homelessness. But for a rich person, taking a risk means maybe this doesn't work out and I try something else. Okay? Completely different games. The poor person is playing not to lose everything. The rich person is playing to multiply everything. Does that make sense? Now, here's where it gets interesting. Let's think about delayed gratification. Okay? Everyone says rich people are good at delayed gratification, right? Rich people can wait for rewards. Poor people want everything now. But think about this more carefully. Who actually delays gratification more? Poor people delay gratification constantly? They skip meals to save money. They wear old clothes for years. They never go on vacation. They never buy anything fun. Their entire life is delayed gratification. Okay. Rich people. Rich people buy what they want when they want it. They go on vacation. They buy nice clothes. They eat at restaurants. So, who's really delaying gratification? Okay. The difference is not willpower. The difference is certainty of return. When a rich person delays gratification, they know the reward is coming. If a rich person invests money, they're confident they'll get returns because they have enough money that even if one investment fails, they have others. Okay? But when a poor person delays gratification, there's no guarantee of reward. If a poor person skips meals to save money, they might save $100 and then their car breaks down and the $100 is gone. So, they sacrificed for nothing. Okay? So, it's not that poor people are bad at delaying gratification. It's that poor people have learned through experience that delaying gratification often leads to no reward. So they're actually being rational when they don't delay gratification. Does that make sense? And this helps us understand something really important. Poor people and rich people are playing two completely different economic games. I call this subtraction versus multiplication. Okay? Poor people play the subtraction game. You start with a little bit of money and every expense subtracts from that money. And the goal is to subtract slowly. Try to make the money last as long as possible. Okay, rich people play the multiplication game. You start with a lot of money and every expense is actually an investment that should multiply your money. And the goal is to multiply as fast as possible. Okay? So, when a poor person's car breaks down, that subtraction minus $500. They're now poorer. When a rich person's car breaks down, they pay $500, but it doesn't affect their wealth at all because their wealth is in assets that are still multiplying. Does that make sense? Or let's think about debt. For poor people, debt is subtraction. It's highinterest credit cards and payday loans. Every month, you're subtracting more and more. You're getting poorer. For rich people, debt is multiplication. It's lowinterest mortgages and business loans. You're using other people's money to buy assets that make you richer. Okay? Same word debt, but completely different meanings, completely different games. And you cannot win a multiplication game using subtraction strategies. Okay. So now let's think about the emergency room decision. If you're rich and you have a minor health issue, like your wrist hurts a little bit, you go to the emergency room immediately. You don't wait. You don't think about the cost. You just go. Why? Because you're playing the multiplication game. Your health is an asset. You invest in maintaining your assets. Okay? But if you're poor and your wrist hurts, you wait. You see if it gets worse. You take some pain medication. You hope it goes away on its own. Why? Because you're playing the subtraction game. Going to the emergency room costs money. Every expense subtracts from your limited resources. So, you wait and you hope you don't have to spend the money. Okay? And we look at this and we say poor people make bad health decisions. But they're not bad decisions. They're rational decisions given the game they're playing. If you're playing subtraction, avoiding expenses is smart. The problem is that sometimes your risk doesn't get better. Sometimes it gets worse. Sometimes waiting turns a minor problem into a major problem. But the poor person was still making the rational choice given their constraints. Does that make sense? Or think about college majors. Rich kid goes to college and their parents say explore, find yourself, try different classes, change your major if you want. This is about discovering your passion. Okay? Why? Because the rich parents are playing the multiplication game. They see college as an investment in their child's network and personal development. The specific degree doesn't matter that much because they know their kid will be fine. They have connections. They have family money. They have a safety net. Okay, poor kid goes to college and everyone tells them, "Pick something practical. Get a degree that guarantees a job. Computer science, engineering, nursing. Don't waste time on art or philosophy or history. You need a job." Okay? Why? Because the poor parents are playing the subtraction game. College costs money. That's a huge subtraction. So, what better result in a highpaying job or the whole thing was a waste? The poor kid can't afford to explore because exploration might lead to no job. And no job means all that money was subtracted for nothing. Does that make sense? So, we have these three fundamental differences. Time horizons, assets versus income, and risk perception. And they all point to the same thing. Poor people and rich people are living in different economic realities. They're playing different games with different rules. And because they're playing different games, advice that works for one group, doesn't work for the other. When a rich person tells a poor person, just invest in index funds, they don't understand that the poor person doesn't have money to invest. When a poor person tells a rich person, you need to budget carefully. The rich person doesn't understand why they would need to budget when their assets generate more than they spend. Okay. So, the question becomes, why does financial advice that works for rich people fail poor people? And the answer is that financial advice assumes you're playing the multiplication game. All financial advice, all the books, all the podcasts, all the courses, they all assume you have surplus money to invest. They assume you can delay gratification and get a reward. They assume you can take risks because you have a safety net. But if you're playing the subtraction game, none of that advice makes sense. Okay. Now, you might be thinking, okay, well, if poor people just learned about wealth, if they understood these concepts, couldn't they escape? And the answer is it's really, really hard. Let me tell you about something I call class defection. Okay, class defection is when a poor person abandons their community and tries to join the rich community. The most common way is marrying into wealth or getting adopted by a rich mentor. Okay? And what happens is you learn by immersion. It's like moving to a foreign country to learn a new language. You're surrounded by people who understand wealth and slowly you start to understand it too. You see how they think. You see how they make decisions. You pick up the cultural codes. Okay, but here's the problem. When you do this, you often lose connection to your origin community. Your family doesn't recognize you anymore. Your old friends think you've changed. You're code switching constantly, acting rich around rich people, acting poor around poor people, and it's exhausting. Okay, I'll give you an example. A poor kid gets a scholarship to an Ivy League school. Suddenly, they're surrounded by rich kids. They learn how rich people talk, how they dress, what they value, how they think about money. The poor kid becomes culturally rich. They learn the language of wealth. But when they go home for Thanksgiving, their family thinks they're acting weird. Why are you talking like that? Why are you so different? The family feels like they lost their kid. And the kid feels like they don't belong anywhere. Not rich enough for rich people, not poor enough for poor people anymore. Okay? And this is why class defection is so rare. It's not just about learning new information. It's about abandoning your entire identity and community. And most people aren't willing to do that. It's too psychologically painful. Does that make sense? So another way of thinking about this is why is it that knowing about money doesn't make you rich? Because wealth isn't about information. It's about circumstance. You can read every book about investing, but if you don't have money to invest, the information is useless. You can understand compound interest perfectly. But if you're living paycheck to paycheck, compound interest doesn't apply to you. Okay. The three things you need to build wealth are money you don't need for survival, time to let investments grow, and psychological safety to take risks. If you don't have all three, you can't build wealth. And poor people by definition don't have these three things. They don't have surplus money. They don't have time because they need returns quickly. They don't have psychological safety because one bad decision means catastrophe. Okay. So when people say why don't poor people just learn about wealth, they're missing the point. Poor people can learn about wealth. They can understand the concepts, but understanding and being able to implement are two different things. It's like explain to someone how to swim when they're drowning. The information is correct, but it's not useful in that moment. Okay, now here's the really interesting part. Why does this system stay stable? Why doesn't it change? And the answer is going to sound crazy, but hear me out. Poor people paradoxically benefit from staying confused about wealth. Okay, let me explain. If a poor person truly understands wealth, truly understands that everything they've been doing is wrong, that they're playing the wrong game, that their entire strategy for life is fundamentally flawed, that's psychologically devastating. Okay? It's much easier to believe, I'm doing the right things. I'm just unlucky. The system is against me, but my decisions are fine. That's a comfortable belief. It protects your ego. It lets you feel like a good person who's just had bad luck. But if you admit that you don't understand wealth, if you admit that you've been playing the wrong game your entire life, that means admitting that you've wasted years, maybe decades, doing the wrong things. And that's too painful. Okay? Plus, there's community reinforcement. Everyone around you thinks the same way. Your family, your friends, your neighbors, they're all playing the subtraction game. They all think having a job is safe. They all think saving cash is smart. They all think rich people just got lucky. So if you suddenly start thinking differently, you're not just rejecting the beliefs, you're rejecting your community. And humans are social creatures. We need community. So most people choose community over truth. Does that make sense? And then there's the admission of waste. If you admit that you don't understand wealth, you're admitting that all those years working hard, all that time spent budgeting, all those sacrifices, they weren't moving you toward wealth. They were just helping you survive poverty. And that's a brutal truth. So, it's psychologically easier to stay confused, to keep believing that you're doing fine. You just need a little more luck. Okay? So, the system stays stable because both groups benefit from the confusion. Rich people benefit because if poor people understood wealth, they'd demand systemic change. They'd say, "Wait, the game is rigged. This isn't fair." But as long as poor people think they're playing the same game as rich people, just losing, they blame themselves instead of the system. Okay? And poor people benefit from staying confused because understanding the truth is too painful. So they maintain their beliefs. They stay in their communities. They keep playing the subtraction game. And they pass these beliefs on to their children. And the cycle continues generation after generation. The rich stay rich because they teach their kids the multiplication game. The poor stay poor because they teach their kids the subtraction game. Okay. So when we ask why do some people build generational wealth while others stay broke forever? The answer isn't about intelligence or work ethic or financial literacy. It's about which game you're playing. And you can't choose which game to play. The game is chosen for you by your birth circumstances. If you're born into a family playing multiplication, you learn multiplication. If you're born into a family playing subtraction, you learn subtraction. and switching games. That requires abandoning everything you know, everyone you love, and admitting that everything you believed was wrong. Very few people can do that. Okay. What do poor people get wrong about how wealth actually works? Everything. Not because they're stupid, but because they're playing a different game where the rules are different. And the tragic thing is that the rules of the subtraction game make perfect sense if you're trying to survive. They just don't build wealth. And nobody tells you that you're playing the wrong game. Everyone just tells you to play harder, play smarter, play better. But you can't win the multiplication game using subtraction strategies. It's impossible. Does that make sense, guys? Okay.
